# MailFang

MailFang is an email testing tool that provides a local SMTP server and a modern web UI to view, inspect, and debug emails during development, in sandboxes, or in staging—without sending messages to external services or real users.

## What MailFang does

- Runs an SMTP server (default port 2525) that accepts mail from your application.
- Stores received emails in a local SQLite database with optional persistence (e.g. Docker volume or configurable path).
- Serves a web UI (default port 3000) where you can:
  - Browse and search emails with field-based search (e.g. subject:, from:, recipient:, text:, html:, attachment:).
  - View rendered HTML, plain text, raw RFC 822 source, and headers.
  - Inspect and download attachments (inline and regular).
  - Preview emails in different viewport sizes (e.g. mobile, tablet, desktop) to test responsive layouts.
  - Toggle whether remote content (images, scripts, etc.) is loaded; by default the UI blocks remote content via Content-Security-Policy for safety.
- Pushes real-time updates to the UI over WebSockets when new mail arrives or when emails are read or deleted, so you see changes immediately without refreshing.

## Problems it solves

- Avoids sending test emails to external providers or real users, keeping data on your own infrastructure and reducing cost and risk.
- Lets you validate content, templates, and behavior locally or in staging before anything reaches production or real inboxes.
- Gives a single, consistent way to capture and inspect mail across frameworks (e.g. Rails, Laravel, Django, Node/Nodemailer) and tools (e.g. swaks) by pointing SMTP at one address and port.
- Reduces reliance on third-party mail APIs or shared test inboxes; you run and control the tool yourself.

## Why MailFang stands out (advantages over similar tools)

- Modern tech stack: Rust backend (Axum, async), Vue 3 + Vite frontend, SQLite. No legacy runtime or heavy language-specific ecosystem; the server is a single, fast binary.
- Realtime experience: WebSockets push new mail and UI state changes instantly. You don’t need to poll or refresh to see new messages.
- Rich, structured search: Query by subject, sender, recipient, body text, HTML content, and attachment filename (e.g. subject:password from:no-reply attachment:invoice.pdf), not just free-text search.
- Responsive preview: Preview emails in multiple viewport presets (e.g. mobile, tablet, desktop) so you can check how templates look on different screen sizes.
- Security by default: Rendered HTML is shown with a strict Content-Security-Policy that blocks remote content (images, scripts, etc.) unless you explicitly allow it, reducing tracking and accidental requests to external servers.
- Standards-oriented SMTP: Implements RFC 5321 and supports common auth methods (PLAIN, LOGIN, CRAM-MD5) and configurable connection limits, so it fits into real-world dev and staging setups.
- Simple deployment: Run via Docker (single image, Alpine-based) or a single static binary; no separate app server or database to install. Optional SMTP auth and persistent SQLite storage via env or CLI.
- Easily configurable: All options (SMTP host/port, web host, database path, SMTP auth, max connections, etc.) can be set via CLI arguments or environment variables, so it fits into scripts, Docker, and CI without config files.
- Full email inspection: Headers, attachments (with inline handling), raw .eml, and rendered HTML/text in one place, with optional remote-content loading for when you need it.
- Self-hosted and private: Everything runs on your hardware; no dependency on a hosted SaaS or external mail service for testing.

For setup, configuration, and examples (Docker, binary, Rails, Laravel, Django, Nodemailer, swaks), see the project’s GitHub repository and the Setup page on this site.
